title: Projects
text:
Landscape Modeling & Spatial Statistics


Mapping Dominant Tree Species Using LiDAR Structural Metrics
This project evaluates whether airborne LiDAR structural metrics can support dominant tree species classification in the Petawawa Research Forest, Ontario, as part of Ontario’s transition toward an Enhanced Forest Inventory (EFI). LiDAR-derived metrics were extracted at ground plot locations and analyzed using mutual information feature selection to identify the most informative structural variables for distinguishing White Pine, Red Pine, and Red Oak.
The analysis integrates spatial data processing, statistical feature ranking, and visualization to assess whether LiDAR-only approaches are sufficient for operational species mapping or whether additional multispectral information may be required.

Spatial Statistics LiDAR Forest Inventory Feature Selection

View project →

title: Projects
text:
Project Management


Assessing Critical Transitions in Agricultural Landscapes in the Lower Fraser Valley
Master’s Capstone Project — University of British Columbia (UBC)
This study examines how agricultural landscapes in the Lower Fraser Valley changed between 2015 and 2020 by harmonizing multi-source land-cover datasets and analyzing landscape structure and land-cover transitions. The project identifies spatial patterns of agricultural change and evaluates opportunities for perennial agriculture expansion within existing agricultural systems.
View full project →



Mapping Spatial Patterns of Urban Carbon Sinks in Metro Vancouver
Research Proposal — UBC GEM 500 (Landscape Ecology)
This project investigates how vegetation distribution and landscape structure influence urban carbon storage across Metro Vancouver municipalities. Using Landsat imagery, carbon biomass datasets, and landscape metrics, the study aims to identify municipalities functioning as major urban carbon sinks and assess the spatial factors influencing carbon storage.
View full project →

title: Oluwaseyi Ezekiel
text: ← Back to Projects

title: Oluwaseyi Ezekiel
text:
Summary
Urban carbon sinks are vegetated areas such as urban forests, parks, street trees, and remnant green spaces that capture and store atmospheric carbon through plant biomass and soil processes. These ecosystems play an important role in climate mitigation by removing carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and storing it in vegetation and soils.
In Metro Vancouver, rapid urban expansion has increased impervious surface cover and fragmented vegetated landscapes, reducing the region’s capacity for carbon sequestration. While many urban forestry assessments focus primarily on canopy cover, fewer studies evaluate how spatial patterns of vegetation and landscape configuration influence carbon storage capacity.
This study proposes to map the spatial distribution of urban carbon sinks across Metro Vancouver municipalities and examine how landscape structure—such as vegetation extent, fragmentation, and connectivity—affects carbon biomass density.
The project addresses two key research questions:

Which municipalities in Metro Vancouver function as the largest urban carbon sinks?
How do spatial patterns of vegetation and impervious surfaces influence carbon storage capacity?

title: Oluwaseyi Ezekiel
text:
Methods
This study integrates remote sensing data, carbon biomass datasets, and landscape metrics to examine spatial patterns of urban carbon storage across Metro Vancouver municipalities.
Carbon biomass density data (tC/ha) and municipal boundaries will be obtained from the Metro Vancouver Open Data Portal, while Landsat 8 and 9 surface reflectance imagery (30 m resolution) will be acquired from the USGS EarthExplorer platform. :contentReferenceoaicite:0
Land-cover classification will be performed in ArcGIS Pro using a supervised maximum likelihood classifier to distinguish three primary classes: vegetated areas, impervious surfaces, and open land.
To evaluate spatial patterns of vegetation, landscape metrics will be calculated using the landscapemetrics package in R, including:

PLAND – proportion of vegetated land cover

Edge Density (ED) – fragmentation between vegetation and impervious surfaces

Largest Patch Index (LPI) – size of the largest contiguous vegetation patch

Patch Density (PD) – distribution of vegetation patches

Finally, multiple linear regression will be used to examine relationships between carbon biomass density and landscape structure across municipalities.

Figure. Proposed workflow for mapping spatial patterns of urban carbon sinks in Metro Vancouver.

title: Oluwaseyi Ezekiel
text:
Expected Results
The study is expected to generate a spatial map of carbon biomass density across Metro Vancouver municipalities.
Municipalities with larger and more contiguous vegetated areas are expected to show higher carbon biomass density, while highly fragmented landscapes dominated by impervious surfaces may exhibit lower carbon storage capacity.
However, vegetation extent alone may not fully explain carbon storage patterns. Differences in vegetation type, canopy structure, and biomass accumulation may produce variation in carbon storage even among areas with similar vegetated cover.

Figure 2. Expected relationship between proportion of landscape vegetated (PLAND) and carbon biomass density.
This analysis will help reveal whether landscape structure and vegetation configuration influence carbon storage potential within urban environments.

title: Oluwaseyi Ezekiel
text:
Project Significance
Understanding spatial patterns of urban carbon sinks is essential for supporting climate mitigation strategies in rapidly urbanizing regions.
This study will help identify:

municipalities with high carbon storage capacity
areas where green infrastructure expansion could increase carbon sequestration
locations where urban restoration efforts may have the greatest environmental impact

The findings can inform urban forestry planning, climate policy, and green infrastructure initiatives aimed at improving ecosystem services and climate resilience across Metro Vancouver.

title: Remote Sensing Projects
text:
← Back to Projects

Remote Sensing Projects
Click a project below to view the full lab report.


Land Cover Classification


Remote Sensing Image Classification Landsat

This project applies supervised image classification to Landsat imagery using the Maximum Likelihood algorithm to map seven land-cover classes: Broadleaf Forest, Coniferous Forest, Exposed Soil and Rocks, High Density Developed, Low Density Developed, Non-forest Vegetation, and Water. The workflow includes training and validation polygons, spectral signature analysis, and accuracy assessment.
See full lab report (PDF) →




LiDAR Forest Structure and Individual Tree Segmentation


LiDAR Forest Structure Point Cloud

This lab uses airborne LiDAR point cloud data to identify and segment individual trees in forest plots. The workflow includes plot extraction, canopy height model generation, tree-top detection, and segmentation using the li2012 and dalponte2016 algorithms, with comparison across multiple canopy height model resolutions to determine an efficient balance between detail and computation.
See full lab report (PDF) →




Spectral Indices and Vegetation Properties


Spectral Analysis Vegetation Indices Remote Sensing

This project analyzes reflectance spectra measured with the ASD FieldSpecPro to compare live vegetation, dead vegetation, soil, and rock. It also computes spectral indices such as PRI, SIPI, and ND705, simulates mixed pixels, and shows how NDVI and NDWI respond to increasing live vegetation cover at Landsat spectral resolution.
See full lab report (PDF) →




Wildfire Burn Severity and Vegetation Recovery


Landsat Time Series NDVI / NBR

This project analyzes wildfire burn severity and post-fire vegetation recovery using a Landsat 8 time series over the Prouton Lakes fire in British Columbia. The workflow includes cloud masking, NDVI and NBR calculation, yearly compositing, dNBR-based burn severity classification, and assessment of vegetation recovery patterns from 2018 to 2021.
See full lab report (PDF) →

Course: Project Proposal Development and Proof of Concept (FCOR 599)
Group project with four MGEM colleagues
Role: Dashboard development and GIS visualization
Team Members

Nathan Mithal

Kate Van den Berg

Muhamad Nafi Andriansyah

Nicole Graham

This ArcGIS Dashboard was developed as part of a simulated citizen science exercise conducted in the UBC MGEM classroom, focused on monitoring birds, mammals, and amphibians. The project was designed to explore the opportunities and challenges associated with citizen-generated environmental data, including issues of data reliability, spatial coverage, and observer bias.



Note: If prompted to sign in to ArcGIS Online, click Cancel to view the dashboard.





Open dashboard full screen →

title: StoryMaps
text:
← Back to Projects

StoryMaps
These StoryMaps are part of my micro-credential in Geospatial Data Collection, Analysis & Interpretation using GIS Technology** at Seneca Polytechnic, Canada. They show how spatial storytelling can be employed in Asset and infrastructure management, Urban planning and other real-world scenarios.



Traffic Analysis and Safety

StoryMaps Traffic Analysis Road Safety

This project analyzes infrastructure distribution and traffic patterns to improve road safety and optimize traffic flow.
View project PDF →


Military Checkpoint Establishment

StoryMaps Site Selection Strategic Planning

This project develops GIS-based solutions for the strategic placement of military checkpoints.
View project PDF →


Field Maps App for Spatial Data Collection

StoryMaps Field Maps Spatial Data Collection

This project demonstrates the use of the ESRI Field Maps app to collect, manage, and communicate spatial data.
View project PDF →


200m Boundary Delineation

StoryMaps Boundary Analysis Real Estate GIS

This project defines a 200-meter jurisdiction boundary for real estate purposes.
View project PDF →
